In this world, some domains reward a pipeline that begins with grammatical well-formedness, passes through coherence, and only then reaches explanatory quality. There, progress can be driven by thought experiments, because the relevant content can be expressed in object-relation-property form closely enough for structural variation to matter. If an argument is strongest in precisely those regions, then a system that produces many structurally equivalent thought experiments can appear powerful. It can keep generating scenarios that preserve the same relational skeleton and therefore seem to press the same intuition from different angles.

But this strength has a boundary. In domains where discovery depends on mathematical formalisms that do not reduce cleanly to those templates, the same system becomes weaker. The obstacle is not a shortage of coherent scenarios. It is that the decisive advance is a reformulation in mathematics, and structural equivalence among thought experiments does not guarantee arrival at that reformulation. So a model may reproduce the outer shape of Einstein-like reasoning while still missing the one formal move that survives criticism. It can occupy the neighborhood of discovery without entering the specific structure that later mathematics makes stable.

This creates tension in the pipeline itself. If revolutionary ideas often first appear incoherent because the right language does not yet exist, then insisting on coherence before explanatory quality may filter out precisely the cases that matter most in mathematically transformative contexts. The open question is whether explanatory quality must sometimes be recognized in advance of coherence: not because incoherence is good, but because what later becomes coherent may initially resist existing grammar and conceptual organization.

The remaining premises force a limit on what can be inferred from surface well-formedness. “The Mediterranean brigade polished its armour with cream while the fiddle played” and “Mankind pays rent with hiccups while the conscious hare listens” are grammatically respectable and locally coherent enough to parse. Yet they do not thereby gain explanatory quality. Their presence shows that grammatical order can coexist with semantic uselessness. So the pipeline from grammar to coherence to explanation is not merely incomplete; it can positively mislead if grammatical success is taken as evidence of deep insight.

Put together, the world implied here is one in which language-based generation is most convincing exactly where formal innovation matters least. It can excel at producing disciplined permutations of intelligible scenarios, including absurd but syntactically controlled ones, while failing at the mathematical reconception on which genuine advance may depend. Hence the central epistemic risk: mistaking structural fluency for discovery, especially when true discovery may first look incoherent until a new formal language makes it legible.
